ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general omelette food product class; no specific crab meat omelette entry exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers egg dishes broadly; no specific crab omelette code exists. Closest parent category used.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of crab meat omelette is approximately 200 g, representing a typical restaurant portion consisting of 3–4 eggs with crab meat filling as served in a Singaporean Chinese seafood restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Egg omelette with seafood — adapted from USDA FoodData Central: Egg omelet or scrambled egg, with seafood (SR Legacy ID 21340) and USDA FoodData Central: Crab, blue, cooked, moist heat (SR Legacy ID 15139)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service, FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for crab meat omelette found in HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN. Values adapted from USDA SR Legacy entry for egg omelet with seafood (ID 21340) as the primary base, cross-referenced with USDA crab meat (blue crab, cooked) for protein and fat adjustment. The dish is approximately 60% egg and 40% crab meat by weight before cooking oil addition. Nutrient values represent a composite estimate per 100 g of the finished dish.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — egg-based dishes and seafood omelette analogues
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement found for crab meat omelette. GI estimated at 32 based on analogous egg omelette entries in the University of Sydney GI Database, which report GI values of 30–35 for plain and seafood egg omelettes. The dish is predominantly egg and crab meat with minimal carbohydrate (1.2 g per 100 g), placing it firmly in the low-GI category. Glycemic load per 200 g serving = (32 × 2.4 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 0.77, rounded to 0.8.

hypertension.guidance: Crab meat omelette contains approximately 380 mg sodium per 100 g (760 mg per standard 200 g serving), which represents about one-third of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size and request reduced soy sauce when ordering.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content and low glycemic index
type2_diabetes.guidance: With only 1.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g and a glycemic index of approximately 32, crab meat omelette has a negligible glycemic load and is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes as part of a balanced meal, though the moderate fat and cholesterol content should be considered in overall dietary planning.

cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Crab meat omelette is high in dietary cholesterol (approximately 310 mg per 100 g from egg yolks and crab) and moderate in sodium; individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume this dish in moderation and be aware that current evidence suggests dietary cholesterol has a variable effect on LDL depending on individual metabolic response.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Crab meat omelette (蟹肉煎蛋, Xie Rou Jian Dan) is a classic Chinese-Singaporean egg dish consisting of beaten whole eggs pan-fried or wok-fried with fresh or cooked crab meat, typically seasoned with soy sauce, sesame oil, white pepper, and spring onion. The dish is cooked in a hot wok or frying pan with oil until the exterior is lightly golden and slightly crispy while the interior remains soft and custardy. The flavour profile combines the rich, savoury taste of egg with the sweet, delicate flavour of crab meat. In Singapore, this dish is commonly served in Chinese seafood restaurants and zi char (煮炒) stalls as a shared side dish or main protein component. Nutritionally, crab meat omelette is a high-protein, low-carbohydrate dish providing approximately 13.2 g protein and only 1.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g, making it suitable for low-carbohydrate dietary patterns. It is a good source of vitamin B12, zinc, and selenium from the crab meat, and provides vitamin A, riboflavin, and choline from the eggs. The dish has a low glycemic index (approximately 32) and negligible glycemic load, making it appropriate for individuals managing blood glucose. Sodium content is moderate to high due to soy sauce seasoning, which is a consideration for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish contains egg and crustacean shellfish allergens and is not suitable for vegetarians or vegans.
common_user_queries: Is crab omelette good for diabetics?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for crab meat omelette was found in HPB Singapore Foodo or ICMR-NIN. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy egg omelet with seafood and USDA crab meat entries. The adaptation is methodologically sound and the composite estimate is consistent with the dish composition, but direct measurement data from a Singapore-specific source would be required for high confidence.
notes: Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy sources (egg omelet with seafood, ID 21340; blue crab cooked, ID 15139) as no exact Singapore-specific entry was found. The dish is classified under Meat and alternatives rather than Dairy and eggs (as in the input) because crab meat is the primary protein component and the dish is served as a protein main. The food_group has been corrected accordingly. Halal status is set to false as crab is not universally accepted as halal in all Islamic jurisprudence schools, though it is consumed by many Muslims in Singapore; this should be verified per specific restaurant certification. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA component sources and are not directly measured for this specific dish. GI of 32 is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database egg omelette entries. No sentinel -1 values remain unexplained; all micronutrient values are positive estimates derived from USDA component data.
